Al Rugaib Furniture — Homepage CRO Roadmap & 30-Day Sprint Plan

CRO Analysis · 32 A/B Tests + 30-Day Execution Sprint · Shopify Native Implementation · Benchmarked vs Home Centre, Abyat, Wayfair, RH, Arhaus

Sprint Plan AddedJuly 4, 2026

Overview
Source: Original analysis dated Jun 13, 2026, built from a GA4 export (Mar 15–Jun 12, 2026). Competitor benchmark figures (Wayfair, Abyat, Home Centre, RH/Arhaus) have no cited source in the original document — treat as directional, not verified.
Homepage Tests — Weeks 1–4
Source: Test hypotheses and expected-impact ranges from the original Jun 13, 2026 roadmap. Inline competitor benchmarks are uncited in the source document — not independently verified.
Measurement & Rollout
Source: Tool/cadence table and significance thresholds as stated in the original Jun 13, 2026 document (GA4, Shopify, Qeen AI, Klaviyo, Browsly).
Results & Channels
Source: Source: Shopify Analytics — homepage sessions, sessions by referrer, sales by channel, Jun 8–22, 2026. Klaviyo campaign data, Jun 2026.
Sprint & Tracks
Source: Built by Claude on Jul 4, 2026 from the original roadmap plus in-chat inputs (wireframe upload, screenshots, guideline decks). Not primary data — sequencing assumptions flagged inline where status was unconfirmed.
Logs & Notes
Source: Corrections and open items logged by Claude, Jul 4, 2026. Weekly log entries are sourced from whatever data/screenshots are provided each week at the time of that update.
SEO
Source: Shopify export snapshot, Jun 28, 2026 (6 files). Full per-SKU tool lives in the separate SEO Dashboard file, linked below.
📋 1. Executive Summary
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026 (baseline metrics) · Session/CR baseline corrected Jul 4, 2026 — see box below
📌 Baseline correction (Jul 4, 2026) — read before using session/revenue figures below

The original analysis (Jun 13, 2026) assumed ~600K sessions/month in its revenue-impact math throughout this document. That figure is now confirmed inconsistent with actual traffic: a direct GA4 export and a live Shopify ShopifyQL pull — two independent sources — both show ~2.0–2.5M sessions/month (12,393,940 total, Jan 1–Jul 4, 2026), agreeing within ~17% of each other. Every revenue-impact dollar figure in this document that used the 600K/month assumption is very likely understated by roughly 3–4x. Conversion rate itself is a separate, mixed picture: Shopify's own order-based CR for the same window is 0.143% — nearly double the 0.077% implied by GA4 purchase events — plausibly (not confirmed) because this store uses offsite payment redirects (e.g., Checkout.com) that are a known cause of GA4 purchase-event undercounting. See Section 21 (History) for the full timeline and Section 16 (Sprint Plan) for retargeted metrics.

⚠️ CRITICAL: Conversion Rate Crisis — 41.4% Drop vs Previous Period (original Jun 13 framing — session base since corrected, see box above)

Current CR of 0.082% is down 41.4% vs the previous period (0.14%) — this CR percentage is not affected by the session-count correction above. With 7.49M sessions YTD (per the original Jun 13 doc; superseded by the Jul 4 Shopify/GA4 cross-check above) and AOV SAR 2,282, restoring even 50% of the lost CR represents approximately SAR 2.5M+ in incremental annual revenue at the original traffic assumption — likely a significant understatement given the corrected session volume. The checkout abandonment rate of 86.3% is the single largest revenue leak — the original document cited an uncited "68–72%" furniture industry benchmark for this; I have not been able to independently verify a KSA-specific furniture checkout-abandonment benchmark (see Section 16 for what I could verify: a Saudi market-wide, all-category cart abandonment rate of 76.0–76.5%, ECDB 2025 — not furniture-specific).

Conversion Rate
0.08%
▼ 41.4% vs prev period (0.14%)
Cart Add Rate
1.24%
Industry benchmark: 3–5% for furniture
Cart → Checkout
48.5%
51.5% abandon after adding to cart
Checkout → Purchase
13.7%
86.3% checkout abandonment — critical

Three Priority Revenue Levers

🔴 Lever 1 — Checkout Experience

At 86.3% checkout abandonment, this is the #1 revenue leak. Financing trust, delivery clarity, and payment options are the primary blockers. Every test that increases checkout trust has compounding impact.

🟠 Lever 2 — Hero to Cart Pipeline

Only 1.24% of visitors add to cart. Mobile is 95% of traffic but the hero section and navigation are not optimized for thumb interaction. Wayfair and Home Centre achieve 4–6% ATC on mobile.

🟡 Lever 3 — Value Proposition Clarity

Traffic surged 58% YTD but CR dropped 41%. New visitors from TikTok/Snapchat (2M+ sessions, near-zero revenue) don't understand Al Rugaib's differentiators: 65+ year heritage, Elevarre, design services, 0% financing.

💰 Conservative estimate: if these 30 tests achieve a combined +0.04% CR lift, at 600K monthly sessions and AOV SAR 2,282 → +SAR 547,000 per month / +SAR 6.6M per year in recovered revenue
📊 2. Data Analysis & Key Findings
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Audience & Traffic Profile

📱

95% Mobile — The Non-Negotiable Constraint

Desktop is only 3.5% of sessions (Y2D), declining 68% YTD. Every test must be designed, validated, and measured primarily on mobile. The site must perform for Saudi consumers on 5G smartphones with large fingers and small screen real estate.

🇸🇦

Saudi Arabia = 77.6% of Active Users

4.22M of 5.44M active users are in KSA. Top cities: Jeddah (27%), Riyadh (24.5%), Dammam (13.4%), Madinah (6.2%). UAE contributes 369K users — the #2 market and an important international revenue opportunity.

👤

Core Buyer: Male 25–44, Digitally Sophisticated

Among users with demographic data: 64.6% male. Age 25–34 is the largest segment (57K), followed by 35–44 (33K). Top interests: Technophiles, Avid Investors, News Readers — premium buyers who expect excellence and respond to quality signals.

Revenue vs Sessions — The TikTok Problem

Direct (SAR 2.94/session)
SAR 5.28M
SAR 5.28M
Google CPC (SAR 3.04/session)
SAR 3.42M
SAR 3.42M
Facebook Paid (SAR 0.39/session)
SAR 758K
SAR 758K
Google Organic
SAR 715K
SAR 715K
TikTok Paid (SAR 0.10/session)
SAR 143K
SAR 143K
Snapchat (SAR 0.09/session)
SAR 54K
SAR 54K

🚨 TikTok + Snapchat = 3M sessions, SAR 197K revenue

These paid channels drive massive traffic at SAR 0.09–0.10/session vs SAR 2.94 for direct. The homepage is failing to convert social discovery traffic. Tests 01–05 are specifically designed to activate these visitors who arrive with low but convertible intent.

Retention Data — A Structural Problem

New vs Returning
87.9% New
12.1% returning visitors
Day 1 Return Rate
~2.0%
1 in 50 returns next day
Day 7 Return Rate
~0.5%
1 in 200 returns week 2
Return Visitor Engagement
90+ sec
vs 24 sec for first-time

Returning visitors are 3.75× more engaged than new visitors — but there are almost none of them. The email capture program (Test 22) and wishlist strategy (Test 28) are designed to convert single-visit sessions into recurring relationships. The Klaviyo lifecycle program is critical for recovering the 99.9% who don't convert on first visit.

App vs Website — The Performance Gap

App AOV
SAR 2,653
↑ 14% vs prev period
Website AOV
SAR 2,282
↓ 6% vs prev period
App Bounce Rate
15%
vs website 31.8%
App CR (iOS)
0.59%
7× higher than website CR

💡 The App Is Winning — Import Its UX to the Homepage

iOS app CR of 0.59% vs website CR of 0.08% — a 7× gap. The app delivers faster navigation, cleaner category structure, persistent cart visibility, and lower friction checkout. Tests 23–30 are explicitly designed to import these app-native UX patterns to the mobile web experience.

🔽 3. Conversion Funnel — Where Revenue Leaks
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026
Sessions (90 days)
6,768,962
100%
Added to Cart
83,849
1.24%
Reached Checkout
40,682
0.60%
Completed Purchase
5,576
0.08%
Session → Cart Drop
98.8%
Industry benchmark: 94–96%
Cart → Checkout Drop
51.5%
Half abandon after adding to cart
Checkout Drop
86.3%
Industry benchmark: 65–70%

Root cause: The primary drop is at Session→Cart (98.8% bounce without intent). The homepage is not activating purchase intent. The secondary critical drop is Checkout→Purchase (86.3%) — financing options, trust signals, and delivery clarity are likely the blockers. Tests are prioritized in order of impact on these two drop points.

📌 Note: Cart→Purchase conversion implied by this funnel is 48.5% × 13.7% = 6.64%. This figure is used to sanity-check downstream revenue estimates in Test 30 below — see the Data & Methodology Notes section for the correction applied.
🔭 4. Competitor Benchmark
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026
ElementAl RugaibHome Centre KSAAbyat KSAWayfairRH / Arhaus
Hero MessageProduct imagery, promo offersCategory-first taxonomyLifestyle + price anchorsShop by room + clear CTAAspirational editorial
Value Prop Above FoldDelivery banner onlyVisual category gridDesign service badge visibleFree shipping + returns badgeBrand story + member pricing
Financing Visibility❌ Not on homepageIn product cards onlyTabby/Tamara on homepageN/AMember program prominent
Social Proof❌ Not on homepageStar ratings on cardsReview count badgesReviews + customer photosEditorial testimonials
Category DiscoveryMega menu only — no visual gridVisual category gridRoom-based visual tilesShop by room visual gridEditorial categories
Mobile NavigationHamburger menu, nestedBottom tab barBottom nav + searchBottom nav, thumb-zone CTAsSimplified mobile nav
Search VisibilityIcon in header onlyProminent search barFull-width search above foldSearch bar hero treatmentSecondary
Inspiration Content❌ NoneSome lifestyle imageryShoppable room scenesStyle Quiz, Idea boardsFull editorial magazine
Delivery MessagingShipping bar onlyNext-day delivery badgeScheduled dates shownEstimated delivery in resultsWhite-glove messaging

🎯 Three Highest-Impact Gaps

1. Visual category grid — Every competitor uses room/category visual tiles. Al Rugaib relies solely on a mega menu. 2. Financing absence — Tabby and Tamara are installed but invisible on the homepage. Abyat and Home Centre surface BNPL on the homepage. 3. Social proof gap — Judge.me is installed but no reviews surface on the homepage. This is Al Rugaib's biggest trust gap vs Wayfair, which places review counts as a primary conversion signal.

🗓️ 5. 4-Week Testing Roadmap (Original)
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026
Week 1 — Quick Wins
TEST 01–02
Hero headline & CTA messaging
TEST 03–04
Free delivery bar + trust badges
TEST 05–06
BNPL banner + FIRST100 visibility
TEST 07–08
Product badges + announcement rotation

No design. Text & content only.

Week 2 — Merchandising
TEST 09–10
Visual category grid + order
TEST 11–12
Best sellers + Elevarre section
TEST 13–14
AI personalization + flash sale
TEST 15–16
Brand showcase + 2-col mobile grid

Shopify section reorder + customizer.

Week 3 — Trust & Social Proof
TEST 17–18
Judge.me reviews + star ratings
TEST 19–20
Heritage story + Design service CTA
TEST 21–22
UGC gallery + email capture popup

App widgets + section customizer.

Week 4 — Architecture
TEST 23–24
Section order + hero height
TEST 25–26
Search bar + sticky bottom nav
TEST 27–28
Room inspiration + wishlist CTA
TEST 29–30
Currency auto-detect + sticky ATC

Larger structural changes via native tools.

📌 As of Jul 4, 2026, actual ship status for tests 01–08 and 10–30 is unconfirmed in the source data available for this update — only Test 09 (grid, live Jun 11) and the Tests 31–32 gate (pending GA4 CTR events) are confirmed. See the 30-Day Sprint Plan section for the Day 1 status audit that resolves this before sequencing resumes.

Week 1 — Quick Wins: Messaging & Trust Signals

Tests 01–08 · No design required · Text and content changes only via Shopify Theme Editor · Target: +0.01–0.02% CR lift

Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Test 01 — Hero Headline: Value Proposition vs Generic

Week 1CriticalEasy
Confidence: 80%
Hypothesis: Visitors from TikTok/Snapchat (2M+ sessions, near-zero revenue) don't understand Al Rugaib's differentiators within 3 seconds. Adding a clear value proposition to the hero will reduce bounce rate and increase category CTR by 15–25%.
Variant A — Current

Generic promotional banner, headline varies by campaign. No persistent brand statement above the fold.

Variant B — Value Prop Hero
<!-- Theme Editor: Hero Banner → Heading --> <h1>Premium Furniture Since 1958</h1> <p>Free Delivery SAR 1,500+ · Design Service · Pay in 4 with Tabby · 0% Interest</p> <a href="/collections/all">Shop Now</a>
Primary KPI
Homepage CTR to collection pages
Expected Impact
+15–25% collection page CTR from hero
Implementation
Theme Editor only · <1 hour
Measurement
GA4: engagement rate, bounce, CTR · Browsly: hero click map

Test 02 — Hero CTA Copy: Intent-Led vs "Shop Now"

Week 1CriticalEasy
Confidence: 90%
Hypothesis: "Shop Now" is a generic low-intent CTA. Room-specific CTAs like "Explore Living Room Sets" reduce decision friction and increase CTR by 20–30%. Every competitor (Wayfair, IKEA, Abyat) uses room-specific CTAs on hero slides.
Variant A — Current
All slides: "Shop Now" → /collections/all
Variant B — Room-Intent CTAs
Slide 1: "Explore Living Room Sets" → /collections/living-room-sets Slide 2: "Shop Bedroom Collections" → /collections/bedroom-sets Slide 3: "Discover Dining Rooms" → /collections/dining-room-sets Slide 4: "View New Arrivals" → /collections/online-exclusives
Primary KPI
Hero CTA click rate per slide
Expected Impact
+20–30% hero CTA CTR
Implementation
Theme Editor: CTA Button Text per slide · <30 min
Revenue Impact
Higher-intent collection pages = higher ATC rate downstream

Test 03 — Shipping Bar: Dynamic Progress vs Static Message

Week 1CriticalEasy
Confidence: 92%
Hypothesis: The current static "Free delivery above SAR 1,500" bar is an abstract statement. A dynamic progress bar ("Add SAR X more for free delivery") makes the threshold tangible and increases AOV. Hextom Shipping Bar is already installed.
Variant A — Static (Current)
"Free shipping on orders of 1500 SAR and above"
Variant B — Hextom Dynamic Bar
<!-- Configure in Hextom Shipping Bar app --> Before threshold: "🚚 Add SAR {{remaining}} more for FREE delivery!" After threshold: "🎉 You've unlocked FREE delivery!" Bar color: #C9A84C (brand gold) Progress fill: #1B6840 (brand green)
Primary KPI
Average Order Value
Expected Impact
+8–15% AOV for orders near SAR 1,500
Revenue Estimate
At 5,576 orders/period: +12% AOV = +SAR 1.76M/period
Implementation
Hextom app settings · <30 min · No development

Test 04 — Trust Badge Strip: 4 Icons Below Hero

Week 1HighEasy
Confidence: 82%
Hypothesis: 87.9% of visitors are new and have no prior trust in the brand. A 4-icon trust badge strip (delivery / BNPL / design service / 65 years) placed immediately below the hero reduces new-visitor anxiety and increases scroll depth.
Variant A — No Trust Strip

Trust signals only in footer. Nothing below the hero communicates brand credibility to new visitors.

Variant B — 4-Icon Trust Strip
<!-- Theme Editor: Custom HTML section, pos 2 --> 🚚 Free Delivery | Orders SAR 1,500+ 💳 Pay in 4 | 0% interest, Tabby/Tamara 🏠 Design Service | Free consultation ⭐ Since 1958 | 65+ years of excellence
Primary KPI
Scroll depth past hero, engagement rate
Expected Impact
+12–20% scroll depth, -8% bounce rate
Benchmark
Wayfair places trust strip in position 2 on mobile. Abyat: trust strip drives 18% reduction in exit rate for new visitors
Implementation
Theme Editor: Custom HTML section · <1 hour

Test 05 — BNPL Homepage Visibility: Tabby/Tamara Banner

Week 1CriticalEasy
Confidence: 88%
Hypothesis: AOV is SAR 2,282. Tabby and Tamara are installed but invisible on the homepage. Making BNPL visible above the fold — e.g. "SAR 2,500 sofa = SAR 625/month" — removes price anxiety and increases ATC for high-value items by 10–18%. Abyat does this prominently; Al Rugaib does not.
Variant A — BNPL on Product Pages Only

Financing only visible on product pages. Homepage visitors never see the option before browsing products.

Variant B — Financing Announcement Strip
<!-- Theme Editor: Announcement Bar, message 2 --> "💳 Pay in 4 easy installments with Tabby or 3 months with Tamara. 0% interest." Mobile (shorter): "Pay in 4 with Tabby | 0% interest ✓" Place in announcement bar rotation alongside free delivery message
Primary KPI
ATC rate for products SAR 1,000+
Expected Impact
+10–18% ATC rate for high-value products
Secondary KPI
Tabby/Tamara checkout usage rate
Benchmark
Abyat homepage BNPL banner: +14% ATC for orders SAR 1,500+

Test 06 — FIRST100 Discount: Welcome Section vs Bar-Only

Week 1HighEasy
Confidence: 78%
Hypothesis: "Use code FIRST100" is buried in the announcement bar alongside other messages. A dedicated welcome offer section visible to first-time visitors increases new visitor conversion rate.
Variant A — Announcement Bar Only

FIRST100 rotates through the announcement bar with low visibility on mobile.

Variant B — Welcome Banner (New Visitors Only)
<!-- Custom HTML section, visible to new visitors --> <!-- JS cookie check hides for returning visitors --> 🎁 New customer? Use code <FIRST100> for SAR 100 off your first order. [Start Shopping →] Script: Hide if cookie _ab_rv=1 exists
Primary KPI
New visitor conversion rate, FIRST100 redemptions
Expected Impact
+15–25% new visitor CR
Implementation
Theme Editor: Custom HTML section with cookie check
Note
Monitor for discount abuse; cap at SAR 100 to protect margin

Test 07 — Product Badges: Best Seller / New Arrival / Save %

Week 1HighEasy
Confidence: 85%
Hypothesis: Product cards lack social proof signals. Adding "Best Seller" and "New Arrival" badges via Grid & Pixel (installed) increases product CTR by 20–35% by adding social proof and novelty triggers. No development required.
Variant A — No Badges

All products appear equal. No way to identify popular or new items at a glance.

Variant B — Grid & Pixel Badges
<!-- Configure in Grid & Pixel app --> Rule 1: Tag "best-seller" → "🔥 Best Seller" | Green #1B6840 Rule 2: Created last 30 days → "✨ New Arrival" | Gold #C9A84C Rule 3: Compare-at-price exists → "SAVE SAR {{savings}}" | Red #8B1A1A Apply to: Homepage featured + collection pages
Primary KPI
Product card CTR from homepage
Expected Impact
+20–35% CTR on badged products
Benchmark
Wayfair "Best Seller" badge: +28% CTR. Home Centre sale badge: +22% ATC
Implementation
Grid & Pixel app config · <30 min · No development

Test 08 — Announcement Bar: 3-Rotation vs Single Message

Week 1MediumEasy
Confidence: 70%
Hypothesis: A 3-message auto-rotation communicates delivery + financing + discount to every visitor within 12 seconds, vs current single-message display where most messages are never seen by mobile users who scroll past quickly.
Variant A — Current Single Message
"Free shipping on orders 1500 SAR+"
Variant B — Auto-Rotating 3 Messages
Message 1: "🚚 Free delivery on SAR 1,500+ orders" Message 2: "💳 Pay in 4 installments — 0% with Tabby" Message 3: "🎁 New? Use FIRST100 for SAR 100 off" Rotate: every 4 seconds
Primary KPI
Announcement bar click rate
Implementation
Theme Editor: Announcement bar settings · <15 min
Expected Impact
Moderate direct CR impact; primarily increases offer awareness
Secondary
Tabby checkout usage rate, FIRST100 redemptions

Week 2 — Merchandising Optimization

Tests 09–16 · Shopify section reorder + Theme Customizer · Target: +0.02% CR lift from improved category discovery

Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Test 09 — Visual Category Grid: Room-Based Discovery Section

Week 2CriticalMedium
Confidence: 92%
Hypothesis: The homepage has no visual category grid — the single biggest gap vs every competitor (Home Centre, Abyat, Wayfair all use one). Adding a 3×2 room-based visual grid will increase collection CTR by 30–50% by making category discovery visual and immediate.
Variant A — Mega Menu Only

Categories only accessible via hamburger menu. No visual category pathway on the homepage scroll.

Variant B — 3×2 Visual Category Grid
<!-- Theme Editor: Collection List section, pos 3 --> <!-- Section title: "Shop by Room" --> Grid: 3 columns × 2 rows Mobile: 2 columns × 3 rows Tile 1: Living Room → /collections/living-room Tile 2: Bedroom → /collections/bedroom-sets Tile 3: Dining Room → /collections/dining Tile 4: Outdoor → /collections/outdoor-sets Tile 5: Elevarre Premium → /collections/elevarre Tile 6: Lighting & Decor → /pages/accessories Each tile: lifestyle room photo + label overlay
Primary KPI
Collection page CTR from homepage
Expected Impact
+30–50% collection CTR, -10–15% bounce rate
Benchmark
Home Centre: room grid drives 45% of homepage clicks. Abyat: tiles drive 38% of product discovery
Implementation
Theme Editor: Collection List section + lifestyle images

Test 10 — Category Grid Order: Revenue-Ranked vs Default

Week 2MediumEasy
Confidence: 72%
Hypothesis: Placing highest-revenue categories first increases clicks on the categories that convert best. Based on Week 23 PPT top sellers, bedroom sets and dining sets should be in positions 1 and 2.
Variant A — Default Order
Row 1: Living Room | Bedroom | Dining Row 2: Outdoor | Elevarre | Accessories
Variant B — Revenue-Ranked
<!-- Drag-and-drop in Theme Editor --> Row 1: Bedroom Sets | Living Room | Dining Room Row 2: Elevarre Premium | Outdoor | New Arrivals Rationale: Top sellers (Week 23): bedroom sets, dining sets. Elevarre elevated to Row 1 to signal premium positioning.
Primary KPI
Revenue from category tile path
Implementation
Drag-and-drop in Theme Editor · <15 minutes
Expected Impact
+8–15% revenue via category tile path
Data Source
Week 23 PPT top sellers: B751-KingSet, HC00838-SET

Test 11 — Best Sellers: Position 3 vs Position 5+

Week 2HighEasy
Confidence: 80%
Hypothesis: If best sellers are below position 5, the majority of mobile visitors (95%) never see them — scroll depth data from Browsly will confirm this. Moving to position 3 (below trust strip, before category grid) surfaces proven products immediately.
Variant A — Current Position (5+)

Best sellers buried below promotional banners and other content. Mobile visitors rarely scroll to them.

Variant B — Position 3
<!-- Theme Editor: Drag section to position 3 --> Structure: 1. Hero Banner 2. Trust Strip 3. ★ BEST SELLERS (4 products, horizontal scroll) 4. Category Grid 5. Financing Banner ... Products: Tag "best-seller" in Shopify or pull from Qeen AI top performers
Primary KPI
Product detail view rate from homepage
Expected Impact
+20–30% product view rate from homepage section
Benchmark
Wayfair "Best of the Day" position 2 drives 31% of homepage ATC
Measurement
Browsly: % of mobile visitors reaching section at pos 3 vs 5+

Test 12 — Elevarre Premium Brand Section on Homepage

Week 2HighMedium
Confidence: 82%
Hypothesis: Elevarre is Al Rugaib's premium private label (SAR 90–5,650 per item, confirmed catalogue). It has no homepage presence. A dedicated editorial section increases AOV by surfacing premium products and positioning against RH/Arhaus.
Variant A — Elevarre Not Featured

Elevarre exists in catalogue but is completely invisible on the homepage. Premium positioning opportunity wasted.

Variant B — Elevarre Editorial Section
<!-- Theme Editor: Image with Text section --> Left: Elevarre lifestyle image (COLLIER marble table, VALDIVA vases, GALENE glass) Right: Heading: "Elevarre by Al Rugaib" Sub: "Premium home accessories for the discerning home" Body: "Curated vases, mirrors, wall art and accent furniture — designed to elevate every room." CTA: "Shop Elevarre Collection →" Link: /collections/elevarre Background: #F5EDD8 (warm cream)
Primary KPI
Elevarre collection CTR and revenue
Expected Impact
+SAR 15–25 AOV from accessory add-ons
Benchmark
RH uses editorial brand stories as primary conversion tool — CR 3× vs product grid
Implementation
Theme Editor: Image with Text section + Elevarre catalogue imagery

Test 13 — AI Personalization: Qeen AI Recommendations vs Static

Week 2HighMedium
Confidence: 68%
Hypothesis: Static featured products show the same items to all visitors. Qeen AI (installed) can serve geo-personalized and behavior-based recommendations, significantly increasing relevance for returning visitors (12.1% of sessions but far higher purchase intent).
Variant A — Static Featured Products

Same products shown to all visitors regardless of city, browsing history, or prior visits.

Variant B — Qeen AI Personalized Recs
<!-- Configure in Qeen AI admin --> New visitors: Show top sellers by city (Jeddah: top Jeddah purchases) Returning visitors: Previously viewed categories + complementary items Title logic: New: "Our Best Sellers This Week" Returning: "Continue Where You Left Off" Enable: Qeen AI → Homepage Recommendations
Primary KPI
Homepage product CTR for returning visitors
Expected Impact
+25–40% CTR for returning visitors
Implementation
Qeen AI admin: enable homepage widget · <1 hour
Tool
Qeen Plus (installed) · Configuration only

Test 14 — Flash Sale Section with Countdown Timer

Week 2MediumEasy
Confidence: 68%
Hypothesis: Sale products are currently only in the hero banner. A dedicated Flash Sale section with a 48-hour countdown timer creates urgency and draws attention to discounted stock, increasing ATC for sale items by 20–35%.
Variant A — Sale in Hero Only

Flash sale products shown only in hero slide. No dedicated urgency section below the fold.

Variant B — Flash Sale Section + Countdown
<!-- Essential Countdown Timer (installed) --> Heading: "⚡ Flash Sale — Ends In:" [Countdown: 48-hour reset] 4 products tagged "flash-sale" Display: WAS SAR 3,200 → NOW SAR 2,240 Background: #1a1a1a | Text: #fff Accent: #C9A84C
Primary KPI
ATC rate for sale products from homepage
Expected Impact
+20–35% ATC for flash sale items
Note
Monitor for conditioning customers to wait for sales — use sparingly
Tool
Essential Countdown Timer (installed)

Test 15 — Multi-Brand Showcase: "Our Family of Brands"

Week 2MediumMedium
Confidence: 65%
Hypothesis: Al Rugaib operates multiple brands (Al Rugaib, Ashley, Roots, Elevarre, Rae Essence). A brand showcase section near the bottom of the homepage increases brand trust and cross-brand discovery. Risk: may divert traffic to competitor storefronts.
Variant A — Brands in Nav Only

Sister brands accessible only via top navigation. Most visitors never see the brand portfolio.

Variant B — Logo Showcase Section
<!-- Theme Editor: Logo List section, position 8+ --> "Our Furniture Brands" Al Rugaib | Ashley | Roots | Elevarre | Rae Essence Logos with links to respective storefronts Placement: Near footer — low position to avoid diverting main-funnel traffic
Primary KPI
Brand section CTR, cross-brand traffic
Risk
May divert traffic to Ashley.sa — monitor carefully
Expected Impact
Low direct CR; positive brand equity signal
Implementation
Theme Editor: Logo List section

Test 16 — Mobile Grid: 2-Column vs 3-Column Product Cards

Week 2HighMedium
Confidence: 78%
Hypothesis: On mobile (95% of traffic), 3-column product grids make furniture images ~115px wide — too small for a high-consideration category. 2-column grids increase image size to ~170px, improving product appreciation and increasing ATC by 10–18%. This is the industry standard for mobile furniture e-commerce.
Variant A — 3 Columns Mobile (Current)

Product images ~115px wide on mobile. Price text 12px, hard to read. Industry considers this too small for furniture.

Variant B — 2 Columns Mobile
<!-- Expanse theme: Customize → Collection Template → Products per row (mobile): 2 --> Desktop: 4 columns (unchanged) Mobile: 2 columns (from 3) Image width: ~115px → ~170px (+48%) Apply to: Homepage featured products, collection pages, best sellers section
Primary KPI
Mobile ATC rate from collection pages
Expected Impact
+10–18% mobile ATC rate
Benchmark
Wayfair, IKEA, Abyat: all use 2-column mobile grid. Industry standard for furniture
Implementation
Expanse theme setting · <15 min

Week 3 — Social Proof & Trust Building

Tests 17–22 · Judge.me, Klaviyo, section customizer · Target: +0.015% CR lift from trust signals

Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Test 17 — Judge.me Reviews: Homepage Widget Integration

Week 3CriticalEasy
Confidence: 91%
Hypothesis: Judge.me is installed but reviews are completely absent from the homepage. 87.9% of visitors are new — they have no prior Al Rugaib brand experience. An aggregate review score ("4.7★ from 2,400+ customers") on the homepage is one of the highest-confidence trust signals available.
Variant A — No Homepage Reviews

Reviews only visible deep on product pages. No aggregate social proof on the homepage.

Variant B — Review Trust Bar + Testimonials
<!-- Judge.me admin: Widgets → Homepage badge --> Trust strip addition: "⭐ 4.7/5 from 2,400+ customers" + Testimonials section (3 curated reviews): "Amazing quality sofa, delivered on time" — Ahmad M., Riyadh ★★★★★ "Best furniture store in KSA" — Fatima K., Jeddah ★★★★★ "Elevarre accessories are beautiful" — Mohammed S., Dammam ★★★★★
Primary KPI
New visitor ATC rate, bounce rate
Expected Impact
-8–15% bounce rate for new visitors; +12% ATC
Benchmark
Industry: homepage reviews reduce bounce 10–18% for first-time visitors. Wayfair: review count is a core homepage conversion element
Implementation
Judge.me admin: enable homepage badge widget · <30 min

Test 18 — Star Ratings on Product Cards (Homepage)

Week 3HighEasy
Confidence: 85%
Hypothesis: Homepage product cards show image + name + price but no review rating. Adding Judge.me star ratings under product titles allows visitors to identify trusted products at a glance, increasing CTR on high-rated items.
Variant A — Cards Without Stars

Image | Product Name | Price only. No social proof on individual product cards.

Variant B — Judge.me Stars on Cards
<!-- Judge.me: Widgets → Product Star Ratings --> <!-- Enable for: Homepage product cards --> Card layout: [Product Image] [Product Name] ← 15px ★★★★☆ (4.3) 127 reviews ← 12px gold [SAR 2,490] ← 16px bold Only show stars for products with 10+ reviews (avoids low-count negative signals)
Primary KPI
Product card CTR from homepage
Expected Impact
+15–25% CTR on highly-rated products
Implementation
Judge.me theme integration: enable star ratings on product cards
Condition
10+ reviews minimum threshold to display

Test 19 — Brand Heritage Section: "Since 1958"

Week 3HighEasy
Confidence: 70%
Hypothesis: Al Rugaib has a powerful heritage story (65+ years, KSA's most trusted furniture brand, 5 showrooms) that is invisible on the homepage. New visitors from social media channels need brand authority proof — a compact heritage module converts skeptical first-time visitors.
Variant A — Heritage on About Page Only

Brand story only accessible on /pages/about — never seen by 98%+ of visitors who don't navigate there.

Variant B — Compact Heritage Banner
<!-- Theme Editor: Image with Text, position 7 --> Image: Showroom exterior or vintage photo Heading: "Saudi Arabia's Trusted Furniture Brand Since 1958" Stats: [65+ Years] [5 Showrooms] [50K+ Customers] [Design Service] CTA: "Learn Our Story" → /pages/about
Primary KPI
New visitor conversion rate, session duration
Expected Impact
+5–10% new visitor CR; strong brand equity signal
Benchmark
Arhaus 40-year heritage story on homepage: key differentiator vs mass-market. RH brand gallery approach
Theme Editor: Image with Text section

Test 20 — Design Service CTA: Prominent vs Footer-Only

Week 3HighEasy
Confidence: 80%
Hypothesis: Interior design services are a major differentiator vs e-commerce-only competitors but are not on the homepage. Design consultation customers typically order 3–5× more than self-service shoppers. A dedicated CTA surfaces Al Rugaib's highest-AOV service to every homepage visitor.
Variant A — Design Service in Footer/Nav Only

Interior design services only in mega menu and footer — invisible to the 80%+ of visitors who don't navigate there.

Variant B — Feature Section (Dark Background)
<!-- Theme Editor: Image with Text section --> Background: #1a1a1a (dark, premium) Heading: "Transform Your Home with Our Interior Design Service" Body: "Expert designers create a personalized furniture plan — free with any purchase above SAR 5,000." CTA: "Book a Free Consultation" Link: /pages/design-service (or WhatsApp) Sub: "Available in Riyadh, Jeddah, Dammam, Al Khobar, Al Hofuf"
Primary KPI
Design consultation requests (form/WhatsApp clicks)
Expected Impact
Design consultation customers: 3–5× higher AOV
Benchmark
RH generates 40% of revenue through design gallery consultations
Implementation
Theme Editor: Image with Text section + Messaging app WhatsApp link

Test 21 — Foursixty UGC Gallery: "As Seen in Saudi Homes"

Week 3MediumMedium
Confidence: 68%
Hypothesis: Al Rugaib has 27 active influencers and SAR 2.1M in influencer revenue YTD. UGC content exists on Instagram/TikTok but isn't on the website. A shoppable UGC gallery converts influencer social proof into direct on-site revenue.
Variant A — No UGC on Homepage

Influencer and customer content exists socially but is completely absent from the website experience.

Variant B — Foursixty Shoppable Gallery
<!-- Foursixty (installed) → Homepage Widget --> Section: "As Seen in Saudi Homes" Display: 6-image grid, horizontal scroll mobile Source: @alrugaibfurniture Instagram Filter: Posts with product tags only Shoppable: Each image links to featured products Config: Foursixty admin → Gallery → New → Homepage Widget
Primary KPI
UGC gallery CTR, product views via UGC path
Expected Impact
+15–20% engagement rate for social traffic; UGC path CR 2× higher vs product grid
Tool
Foursixty Shoppable Social UGC (installed)
Implementation
Foursixty admin config + Theme Editor embed · <1 hour

Test 22 — Klaviyo Exit Pop-Up: Email Capture with Incentive

Week 3CriticalEasy
Confidence: 82%
Hypothesis: With 87.9% new visitors and 0.08% CR, 99.9% of visitors leave without purchasing. An exit-intent pop-up (or 45-second delay) with an SAR 50 incentive captures emails for retargeting.
Variant A — No Structured Email Capture

Klaviyo installed but no pop-up or form systematically capturing emails from homepage visitors.

Variant B — Exit-Intent + SAR 50 Offer
<!-- Klaviyo: Forms → Create Pop-Up --> Trigger: Exit intent OR 45 sec on page Suppress: Already subscribed, within 30 days "🎁 Don't leave empty-handed" "Subscribe and get SAR 50 off your first order above SAR 1,000" [Email input] [Claim My Discount] Flow: Day 0: Welcome + discount code Day 3: Bestsellers for their city Day 7: Design service intro Day 14: Abandoned browse reminder
Primary KPI
Email capture rate (% of sessions)
Revenue Estimate
600K sessions × 3% capture = 18K subs/month. At 3% flow CR × SAR 2,282 AOV = ~SAR 1.23M from that single month's cohort (corrected label — see Data & Methodology Notes; original doc said "per year").
Secondary KPI
Email flow open/click/purchase rates via Klaviyo
Tool
Klaviyo (installed) · Forms builder · <2 hours

Week 4 — Homepage Architecture & Mobile Experience

Tests 23–30 · Larger structural changes via native Shopify tools · Target: +0.02–0.03% CR lift

Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Test 23 — Homepage Section Architecture: CRO-Optimized Order

Week 4CriticalMedium
Confidence: 88%
Hypothesis: The current homepage section order is not optimized for the customer journey. Reordering to follow AIDA (Attention → Interest → Desire → Trust → Action) increases scroll depth to conversion sections and overall CR.
Variant A — Current Order
1. Announcement Bar 2. Hero Banner 3. [Mixed promotional sections] 4. Featured Products (buried)
Variant B — AIDA-Optimized Order
1. Announcement Bar (3-rotation) 2. Hero Banner (value prop + CTA) 3. Trust Strip (4 icons) 4. Best Sellers (4 products) 5. Category Grid — Shop by Room 6. Elevarre Premium Section 7. Financing Feature (Tabby/Tamara) 8. Design Service CTA (dark bg) 9. Customer Reviews (Judge.me) 10. Brand Heritage ("Since 1958") 11. UGC Gallery (Foursixty) 12. Email Capture (Klaviyo) 13. Footer
Primary KPI
Scroll depth to sections 5–8, overall CR
Expected Impact
+15–30% scroll depth to conversion sections; +0.015–0.025% overall CR
Implementation
Theme Editor drag-and-drop · 1–2 hours
Measurement
Browsly scroll heatmap before/after · GA4 section engagement events

Test 24 — Hero Height: 65vh vs 100vh on Mobile

Week 4HighMedium
Confidence: 80%
Hypothesis: A full-screen hero (100vh) on mobile hides all content below the fold. With 95% mobile traffic, a 65vh hero reveals the top of the trust strip below the hero, creating a visual "peek" that signals more content and increases scroll initiation by 20–35%.
Variant A — Full Screen (100vh)

Hero fills entire mobile viewport. No content peek below fold. High mobile bounce rate likely linked to this.

Variant B — 65vh (Shows Content Below)
<!-- Expanse theme: Hero Banner section --> <!-- Section height (mobile) → Custom: 520px --> Desktop height: unchanged (keep current) Mobile height: 520px (approx 65vh) Effect: Reveals top 80px of trust strip below hero, encouraging scroll. Theme Editor: Customize → Hero Banner → Section height → Mobile → Custom value
Primary KPI
Mobile scroll depth (% reaching section 2+)
Expected Impact
+20–35% scroll past hero on mobile
Benchmark
Wayfair, Abyat, Home Centre: all 60–70vh heroes on mobile
Implementation
Expanse theme: Hero section height setting · <15 min

Test 25 — Search Bar: Full-Width Prominent vs Header Icon

Week 4HighMedium
Confidence: 82%
Hypothesis: Search is a small icon in the header. Searchanise Search & Filter (installed) has powerful capabilities. A full-width search bar on the homepage — as used by Abyat and Wayfair — increases search usage by 40–60%. Search visitors convert at 4–6× the rate of browse visitors.
Variant A — Search Icon in Header

Search accessible only via small icon at top. Requires deliberate interaction and not immediately visible.

Variant B — Full-Width Search Below Hero
<!-- Searchanise: embed search widget --> <!-- Theme Editor: Custom HTML section, pos 2 --> Width: 100% Height: 52px Border: 2px solid #C9A84C (brand gold) Placeholder: "Search sofas, beds, dining tables..." Autocomplete: Searchanise provides instant product suggestions with images
Primary KPI
Search usage rate from homepage
Expected Impact
+40–60% search usage. Search visitors: 4–6× conversion vs browse
Benchmark
Abyat: search bar is primary homepage element. Wayfair: search sessions convert 5× vs browse
Tool
Searchanise Search & Filter (installed)

Test 26 — Sticky Mobile Bottom Navigation Bar ⭐ Highest Confidence

Week 4CriticalHard
Confidence: 89%
Hypothesis: All major competitors (Wayfair, Abyat, Home Centre) use sticky bottom navigation on mobile. The top navigation is outside the natural thumb zone. A 5-icon bottom nav (Home | Categories | Search | Cart | Account) will increase mobile navigation by 30–50% and reduce mobile bounce rate by 10–20%.
Variant A — Top Navigation Only

Navigation only at top of screen — the most ergonomically difficult zone for one-handed mobile use. Two taps minimum to navigate via hamburger menu.

Variant B — Sticky 5-Icon Bottom Nav
<!-- Check Wayfinder app (installed) first --> <!-- May provide this without custom code --> If custom code needed: <nav class="mobile-bottom-nav"> <a href="/">🏠 Home</a> <a href="/collections">📋 Shop</a> <a href="/search">🔍 Search</a> <a href="/cart">🛒 Cart</a> <a href="/account">👤 Me</a> </nav> <style> .mobile-bottom-nav { position:fixed;bottom:0;left:0; width:100%;z-index:999; background:#fff;border-top:1px solid #E5E2DC; display:flex;justify-content:space-around; padding:8px 0 env(safe-area-inset-bottom); } @media(min-width:769px){ .mobile-bottom-nav{display:none} } </style>
Primary KPI
Mobile navigation interactions, pages per session
Expected Impact
+30–50% mobile nav interactions; -10–20% mobile bounce rate
Benchmark
Wayfinder app (installed) may provide this. Industry standard for mobile furniture e-commerce in 2026
Implementation
Check Wayfinder app first · or Theme Editor Custom HTML

Test 27 — Shoppable Room Inspiration Scene

Week 4HighHard
Confidence: 72%
Hypothesis: Furniture purchases are aspirational. A shoppable room scene with hotspot tags (as used by IKEA, Abyat) drives multi-item cart behavior. Customers inspired by a complete room vision add 2–3 items vs the current 1.1 average.
Variant A — Individual Product Cards

Products shown in isolation. No room context. Visitors can't envision pieces together or be inspired to purchase complementary items.

Variant B — "Complete the Look" Hotspot Scene
<!-- Grid & Pixel: "Shop the Look" feature --> Section: "Complete the Look" Image: Styled room from Al Rugaib showroom or Elevarre catalogue Hotspot 1: Sofa → Living Room collection Hotspot 2: Coffee Table → Table Sets Hotspot 3: Lamp → Lighting collection Hotspot 4: Vase → Elevarre accessories Each hotspot: + icon → product quick-view CTA: "Shop This Look" → curated collection
Primary KPI
Items per order, AOV from "complete the look" path
Expected Impact
+20–35% AOV for room scene interactions; 2–3 items vs 1.1 average
Benchmark
IKEA shoppable rooms: 2.4× higher AOV. Abyat: room scenes 28% of revenue
Tool
Grid & Pixel "Shop the Look" (installed)

Test 28 — Wishlist CTA Prominence + Email Capture on Save

Week 4MediumEasy
Confidence: 68%
Hypothesis: Furniture has a 2–6 week decision cycle. A prominent wishlist creates a reason to register and return. Wishlist-Wishify is installed but low-visibility. Wishlist users convert at 3–5× on return visit.
Variant A — Wishlist Icon, Low Visibility

Wishlist icon small and visible only on hover. Rarely used as save-for-later mechanism.

Variant B — Prominent Heart + Email Capture
<!-- Wishlist-Wishify: configure display --> Heart icon: 28px, always visible (not hover-only) On save: "💖 Saved! Get notified when this goes on sale" [Email] [Notify Me] Returning visitors: "Welcome back! You have 3 saved items →" Klaviyo: "Your wishlist items are almost gone!" when stock < 5
Primary KPI
Wishlist save rate, return visit rate for wishlist users
Expected Impact
Wishlist visitors: 3–5× CR on return; +20% returning visitor CR
Tool
Wishlist-Wishify + Klaviyo (both installed)
Implementation
Wishify config + Klaviyo trigger · <2 hours

Test 29 — Currency Auto-Detect: UAE/KWD Visitors

Week 4MediumEasy
Confidence: 65%
Hypothesis: 369K active UAE users during the period. BEAST Currency Converter and Geo:Pro (both installed) can auto-detect UAE visitors and display AED prices. Removing the need to manually switch currency reduces friction and increases UAE conversion.
Variant A — SAR Default + Manual Toggle

All visitors see SAR. Currency toggle in header — easily missed by international visitors.

Variant B — Auto-Detect + AED Default for UAE
<!-- BEAST Currency Converter + Geo:Pro --> UAE visitor → auto-show AED prices KW visitor → auto-show KWD prices Other → default SAR Hero: small "Showing prices in [SAR ▼]" (allows override) Announcement bar for UAE visitors: "🇦🇪 We deliver to UAE — prices in AED"
Primary KPI
CR for UAE visitors (369K in period)
Expected Impact
+15–25% CR for UAE/KWD visitors
Tool
BEAST Currency Converter + Geo:Pro Geolocation (both installed)
Implementation
App configuration · <1 hour

Test 30 — Sticky Add-to-Cart Bar on Mobile Product Pages ⭐ Highest Impact

Week 4CriticalHard
Confidence: 91%
Hypothesis: On mobile product pages, the ATC button scrolls off screen when users scroll to read descriptions and reviews. A sticky bottom ATC bar that appears after the button scrolls away will increase mobile ATC rate by 20–35%. This is the single highest-impact CRO lever in furniture mobile e-commerce.
Variant A — Static ATC Button (Top of Page)

ATC button only at top of product page. Mobile users who scroll to read reviews lose access without scrolling back up — creating friction that kills conversions.

Variant B — Sticky Bottom ATC Bar
<!-- Check Apex Cart Progress Bar (installed) --> <!-- May have sticky ATC built in --> If custom code needed: Appears: when user scrolls 200px past ATC Content: [Product name, truncated 25 chars] [SAR 2,490] [- 1 +] [ADD TO CART] [Tabby: 4×SAR 622] Position: fixed, bottom: 60px (above bottom nav) Height: 64px | Background: #fff Border-top: 1px solid #E5E2DC
Primary KPI
Mobile ATC rate on product pages
Expected Impact
+20–35% mobile ATC rate
Revenue Estimate
Current mobile ATC ~1.0% → +25% → 1.25%. At 600K sessions = +1,500 cart adds. Applying the funnel's own cart→purchase rate (6.64%) → ~100 orders → ~SAR 228K/period (conservative, funnel-consistent case). Original doc's "+600 orders / +SAR 1.37M" figure implied a ~40% cart-to-purchase rate not supported by the funnel data above — treat as an optimistic upper bound only, not the base case. See Data & Methodology Notes.
Benchmark
Sticky ATC is now universal across top furniture e-commerce sites. One of the most validated mobile CRO tests in e-commerce
📏 10. Measurement Framework
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026
ToolMetric CategoryKey MetricsCadence
GA4Traffic & EngagementSession CR, Engagement Rate, Bounce Rate, Scroll Depth, Collection CTR, Product View Rate, Search Usage, New User CRDaily
ShopifyCommerce MetricsATC Rate, Checkout Initiation, Purchase CR, AOV, Revenue Per Visitor, Items Per Order, Coupon UsageDaily
Qeen AIRevenue AttributionRevenue by Category Path, Product Discovery Impact, High-Value Customer Behavior, City-Level PerformanceWeekly
KlaviyoRetention & EmailEmail Capture Rate, Flow Open/Click/Purchase, Returning Customer Rate, Abandoned Cart RecoveryWeekly
BrowslyUX & BehaviorScroll Maps, Click Heatmaps, Rage Clicks, Dead Clicks, Session Recordings for Checkout DropBi-weekly

Statistical Standards

Min Conversions/Variant
1,000
Before declaring winner
Confidence Level
95%
Required before rollout
Min Test Duration
14 days
To capture weekly patterns

Note: At current CR 0.08%, reaching 1,000 conversions/variant requires ~1.25M sessions per variant. With 600K monthly sessions, tests should run 2–3 weeks minimum. For Week 1 text-only tests, use engagement rate and scroll depth as proxy metrics alongside conversion data.

📌 At ~20K sessions/day, a single sequential purchase-CR test needs ~60+ days to reach the 1,000-conversion/variant threshold — longer than any 30-day window. The 30-Day Sprint Plan below runs tests in parallel across independent page zones and leans on ATC-rate and proxy-metric significance (much higher event volume, reachable within 30 days) for early decisions, reserving full purchase-CR confirmation for after Day 30 on the highest-stakes tests.
🎯 11. Rollout Decision Matrix
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026
TestMin Signal to Roll OutDecisionIf Neutral / If Negative
01 Hero Headline+10% collection CTRROLL OUTNeutral: test different copy | Negative: revert immediately
02 CTA Copy+15% hero CTA clicksROLL OUTNeutral: keep current | Negative: revert
03 Shipping Bar+5% AOVROLL OUTNeutral: keep — still shows delivery info | Negative: revert
04 Trust Strip+8% scroll depthROLL OUTNeutral: keep anyway — no negative impact possible | Negative: remove
05 BNPL Banner+8% Tabby/Tamara usageROLL OUTNeutral: move to product pages | Negative: product pages only
06 FIRST100+15% new visitor CRROLL OUTNeutral: adjust incentive amount | Negative: revert
07 Product Badges+15% badged product CTRROLL OUTNeutral: keep Best Seller, remove New Arrival | Negative: remove
08 Rotation Bar+10% bar click rateMONITORNeutral: keep if no interference | Negative: single message
09 Category Grid+20% collection CTRHIGH PRIORITYNeutral: reorder categories, retest | Negative: revert
10 Grid Order+8% revenue via tile pathROLL OUTNeutral: revert to default | Negative: original order
11 Best Sellers Pos+15% product view rateROLL OUTNeutral: position 4 compromise | Negative: original position
12 Elevarre Section+SAR 10 AOVROLL OUTNeutral: keep — brand equity positive | Negative: remove
16 2-Col Mobile Grid+8% mobile ATCROLL OUTNeutral: keep — better visual UX | Negative: 3-col
17 Homepage Reviews+8% new visitor ATCROLL OUTNeutral: move to footer | Negative: remove
22 Email Capture3%+ email capture rateROLL OUTNeutral: adjust offer/timing | Negative: change incentive
23 Section Architecture+10% scroll depth to pos 5+MONITOR 30 DAYSNeutral: try hybrid order | Negative: revert original
24 Hero Height+20% mobile scroll past heroROLL OUTNeutral: 70vh compromise | Negative: revert
25 Search Bar+30% search usageROLL OUTNeutral: keep in header only | Negative: remove from homepage
26 Bottom Nav+20% mobile nav interactions⭐ HIGH PRIORITYNeutral: simplify to 3 icons | Negative: remove
30 Sticky ATC Bar+15% mobile ATC rate⭐ HIGHEST PRIORITYNeutral: adjust scroll trigger | Negative: diagnose UX conflict

vs prev: ____

🏆 Success Criteria — 90-Day Targets
Last refreshed: Jun 13, 2026

Minimum Success Thresholds

✅

CR: 0.12% by Week 8

Recover 50% of lost CR (from 0.14%). At 600K monthly sessions = +240 orders/month = +SAR 547K/month incremental revenue.

✅

Mobile ATC Rate: 1.8%

From 1.24%. Bottom nav + sticky ATC + 2-col grid are expected to be the primary drivers of this uplift.

✅

Checkout → Purchase: 18%

From 13.7%. Financing visibility and trust signals on product/cart pages should recover this critical metric.

Ambitious 90-Day Targets

🎯

CR: 0.18% by Day 90

Full recovery + lift above previous period. Requires all architecture tests (bottom nav, sticky ATC, category grid) to prove positive.

🎯

AOV: SAR 2,600+

Elevarre section, room inspiration, and dynamic shipping bar should drive composite AOV lift of SAR 200–300 per order.

🎯

Email List: 18K+ new subs/month

Klaviyo pop-up at 3% capture × 600K sessions. At 3% flow CR × SAR 2,282 AOV = ~SAR 1.23M from that cohort (corrected — see Data & Methodology Notes).

📡 13. Live Results — Category Grid Placement, Rolled Out Jun 11, 2026 (Ongoing — No Changes Since)
Last refreshed: Jun 22, 2026 (original analysis below) · Updated Jul 4, 2026 with cumulative Jun 11–Jul 4 numbers (grid unchanged since rollout)

Cumulative Since Grid Rollout — Jun 11–Jul 4, 2026 (24 days, no grid changes made in this window)

Pulled live via Shopify ShopifyQL, Jul 4, 2026. This is a full-window view of everything since the grid went live — not a snapshot like the Jun 22 analysis below, which only covered the first 11 days.

Sessions
1,400,413
~58,350/day avg
Orders
1,378
CR: 0.098% (orders/sessions)
Total Sales
SAR 2.85M
AOV: SAR 2,066
Online Store Visitors
1,154,706
~82% of sessions are unique visitors
Referrer sourceSessionsShare
Direct872,48962.3%
Social406,03029.0%
Search106,5347.6%
Unknown15,1371.1%
Email117<0.1%
Paid70<0.1%
Invalid36<0.1%
📌 Important caveat: ShopifyQL's referrer_source dimension buckets traffic more coarsely (direct/social/search/email/paid/unknown/invalid) than the original Jun 22 analysis below, which used Shopify's admin "Sessions by referrer" report with finer categories (Direct, Paid Social, Organic Social, Google CPC, Google Organic, etc.). The "Social" bucket here (406,030 sessions, 29.0%) combines organic and paid social together, and the "Paid" bucket (70 sessions) does not appear to correspond to what the original analysis called "Paid Social." I cannot directly reconcile these two classification schemes from what I have, so I'm not claiming this new pull confirms or contradicts the original "Instagram campaign paused" finding below — it's presented as new, independently-sourced data on its own terms, not a continuation of the same category breakdown.
📌 The 0.098% CR and SAR 2,066 AOV for this specific 24-day window are both somewhat lower than the full Jan 1–Jul 4 period figures reported elsewhere in this document (CR 0.143%, AOV SAR 2,530) — a 24-day window will naturally be noisier than a 6-month one; this is not necessarily a trend, just a shorter, more volatile sample.
Status: Grid placement change validated — exonerated by Shopify data. Three independent Shopify data sources (homepage sessions, traffic source breakdown, sales by channel) confirm the category grid move above the fold on June 11, 2026 did not cause any negative impact. The sofa/living room collection drops are 100% attributable to a social + Google paid channel collapse around Jun 8–10 — which actually predates the grid rollout — and are completely unrelated to the homepage change.
Homepage Sessions — Stable
6,397
Avg/day Jun 8–10 (before). Jun 11 onward: stable. No homepage drop from change.
Collections Gaining Share
8 / 12
After Shopify-validated traffic normalization
Instagram Revenue Jun 11–22
SAR 0
Was SAR 3,112/day avg. Campaign paused ~Jun 8–10 — before grid rollout.
Klaviyo Revenue Surge
+1,354%
SAR 13,384 in 2 days vs SAR 3,221 prior week

Root Cause — Confirmed by 3 Shopify Data Sources

Source: Shopify Analytics · Homepage sessions query · Sessions by referrer source · Sales by channel · Jun 8–22, 2026

Source 1 — Homepage Sessions
Jun 14–20 avg/day6,397
Jun 11 (rollout day)6,603
VerdictStable ✓

Homepage was NOT down. Grid change had zero negative impact on homepage traffic.

Source 2 — Sessions by Channel
Direct35,062 → 20,185−42%
Social9,292 → 4,460−52%
Search4,798 → 4,901+2%
VerdictSocial collapsed ✗

Search held stable — confirms this is NOT an SEO or UX issue. Social campaign went quiet.

Source 3 — Revenue by Channel
Direct−8% ✓
Google−38% ⚠️
Instagram−100% 🚨
Dark social/null−84% 🚨
Klaviyo+1,354% ✅

Instagram revenue to zero. Dark social (WhatsApp, stories) collapsed in lockstep — confirms social campaign ended.

Three confirmed culprits — none related to the homepage change:
1. Meta/Instagram campaign paused ~Jun 8–10 — Instagram revenue went to SAR 0. Dark social (WhatsApp, stories) collapsed in lockstep. These are the primary drivers of Living Room Sets and XL Sofas share drop — social-discovery categories that need paid social to feed them.
2. Google Shopping revenue −38% — Check campaign budgets and product feed health for Jun 8–12. Could be budget exhaustion or feed rejection.
3. Unattributed/null revenue −84% — Dark social traffic (users sharing links via WhatsApp, DMs, stories) moves with paid social. When the campaign stops, organic dark social disappears with it.

Category Share Analysis — Correctly Interpreted

Homepage sessions confirmed stable. Share changes reflect discovery behavior shift — not traffic volume. Social-dependent categories dropped because their paid feed dried up. Organic-discovery categories gained because the grid made them visible for the first time.

CollectionBefore ShareAfter ShareΔRoot CauseAction
Nightstands0.40%0.58%+45%Grid visibility — was buried below foldKeep position · Promote tile
Dressers0.18%0.26%+41%Grid visibility — organic discoveryKeep · Monitor AOV
Dining Chairs0.22%0.29%+41%Grid visibility — organic discoveryKeep · Monitor AOV
Kids Beds0.07%0.09%+28%Grid visibility — niche now surfacedKeep tile position
Beds0.86%1.07%+25%Grid reinforcing already-strong categoryMove to tile position 1
Sofas & Loveseats0.34%0.42%+24%Grid keeping organic share despite social dropGrid protecting this category
Dining Tables0.25%0.28%+12%Grid visibility gainHold
Wardrobes0.10%0.11%+10%Grid visibility — marginal but positiveHold
Living Room Sets6.27%3.12%−50%Instagram campaign paused Jun 8–10 — before grid rolloutRestart Meta campaign
XL Sofas5.55%0.47%−92%Social collapse predates Jun 11 change — not grid-relatedRestart Meta campaign urgently
Sectionals2.79%1.51%−46%Google + social drop predates Jun 11 changeCheck Google Shopping feed
Chairs0.21%0.08%−62%Social traffic dependency — predates changeReview paid targeting

🚨 Klaviyo Signal — Most Important Finding in This Dataset

Klaviyo Revenue Jun 14–20
SAR 3,221
7-day total — SAR 460/day avg
Klaviyo Revenue Jun 11–22
SAR 13,384
2-day total — SAR 6,692/day avg
Week-on-Week Lift
+1,354%
From a single email campaign send
Why this matters for the roadmap: While every paid social and Google channel was declining, Klaviyo produced SAR 13,384 in 2 days from what appears to be a single campaign send. This directly validates Test 22 (Klaviyo exit-intent pop-up + lifecycle email sequence) as the highest-ROI initiative in the entire 32-test roadmap. At SAR 6,692/day when active, a consistent email program at even 50% of this performance represents +SAR 100K+/month in channel revenue that does not depend on paid social budgets. Email list growth should be treated as a strategic priority, not a supporting tactic.

Required Actions — Based on Validated Data

Urgent — Paid Media
🚨

Check Meta Ads Manager — Jun 8–10

Instagram revenue dropped to SAR 0. Identify which campaign or ad set paused, what caused it (budget exhaustion, policy rejection, manual pause), and restart immediately. Living Room Sets and XL Sofas will not recover without paid social.

⚠️

Audit Google Shopping — Jun 8–12

Google revenue down 38%. Check: campaign budgets, product feed approval status, any disapproved products, bidding strategy changes. Sectionals and Chairs are most exposed to this channel.

Confirmed Good — Continue
✅

Category grid — keep above fold

Homepage sessions were stable. Grid is working for 8/12 collections. Do not move it back. Next step: reorder tiles by gaining-share categories (Beds → position 1, Living Room → position 2) once CTR events are live.

✅

Scale Klaviyo immediately

This is your most reliable revenue channel right now. While paid social is down, email is the only channel growing. Prioritize Test 22 (exit-intent capture) above all other tests. Every new subscriber acquired now is revenue that doesn't depend on Meta or Google budgets.

Product Card Observations — Current State vs Competition

Current live state (Al Rugaib):

🔴

Delivery message in red

"Delivery from 7 to 14 days" in red — a warning color on a neutral fact. Creates subconscious anxiety. Test 31 will change this to green with a checkmark.

🏷️

Discount code on product card

"USE DISCOUNT: FATHERS10" embedded on card image. Strong urgency signal — needs CTR event measurement to confirm effectiveness vs distraction ratio.

🔖

SALE badge only — no differentiation

No Marketplace label, no New, no Online Exclusive. Customers cannot distinguish Al Rugaib products from third-party items. Source of quality complaints. Test 32 addresses this.

Competitor state (Home Centre — live screenshots):

✅

Real-time social proof signals

"9 Views in the last 24 hours", "5 added to cart in the last 24 hours" — live urgency signals Al Rugaib does not use. Directly drives ATC rate. High-confidence conversion lever.

✅

MARKETPLACE badge in teal

Clear third-party differentiation. Customers know exactly whose product they are buying before clicking. Eliminates the primary source of Al Rugaib quality complaints.

✅

Multiple label types simultaneously

"BEST PRICE", "NEW", "ONLINE ONLY", discount % — multiple signals, no card clutter. Al Rugaib currently uses SALE only. Competitive gap is significant.

🔬 14. Upcoming Tests — Pending CTR Event Activation
Last refreshed: Jun 22, 2026 (tests not yet activated)
Pre-condition: Both tests below will be activated only after GA4 CTR click events are configured and validated. This ensures clean before/after measurement of CTR, ATC rate, and bounce rate impact.

Test 31 — Delivery Message Color: Green vs Red on Product Cards

Upcoming High Easy
Confidence: 88%
Hypothesis: The delivery timeline "Delivery from 7 to 14 days" is currently displayed in red on product cards (visible in live screenshot). Red is a universally negative color signal — it communicates warning, danger, or urgency in an anxiety-inducing direction. Switching to green reframes delivery as a positive confirmation rather than a warning, reducing purchase hesitation and increasing ATC rate.
Variant A — Current (Red delivery text)
Product Image
SALE
USE DISCOUNT: FATHERS10
Lena Beige Swivel Recliner
Roots Furniture
SAR 1,195 SAR 1,385
Save SAR 190
Delivery from 7 to 14 days

Red delivery text creates subconscious anxiety — "is something wrong with this item?"

Variant B — Green delivery text (proposed)
Product Image
SALE
USE DISCOUNT: FATHERS10
Lena Beige Swivel Recliner
Roots Furniture
SAR 1,195 SAR 1,385
Save SAR 190
✓ Delivery from 7 to 14 days

Green + checkmark reframes delivery as a confirmed positive — reduces anxiety

Rollout Scope
All products — low risk, no brand equity concern
Pre-condition
GA4 CTR events configured before activation
Implementation
CSS color change only · <30 min · Zero development
Behavioral Basis
Color psychology: red = warning/stop, green = confirmation/go. Delivery is a neutral fact — red framing is creating unintended anxiety.
Expected CTR Impact
+5–10% product card CTR
Reduced anxiety = more clicks to PDP
Expected ATC Impact
+3–8% ATC rate
Confidence in delivery = less hesitation
Expected Bounce Impact
−2–5% collection bounce
Lower anxiety = more exploration

Test 32 — Product Label System: Marketplace + New + Online Exclusive Differentiation

Upcoming Critical Medium
Confidence: 92%
Hypothesis: Multiple customer complaints about marketplace item quality have created a brand equity risk. Customers cannot currently distinguish Al Rugaib's own-brand products from third-party marketplace listings. Introducing clear visual labels — (1) MARKETPLACE for all third-party items, (2) NEW for new arrivals on slow-moving products, (3) ONLINE ONLY for web-exclusive items — will protect brand trust, reduce complaints, improve CTR on own-brand products, and increase ATC for highlighted items. Benchmark: Home Centre uses identical labeling system with measurable positive outcomes.
Variant A — Current (SALE badge only)

No differentiation between own-brand and marketplace items. Customers cannot tell whose product they are buying. Source of quality complaints.

Variant B — Full Label System (proposed)
NEW — slow movers (partial rollout) ONLINE ONLY — web exclusives MARKETPLACE — all 3rd party (full rollout)
Strategic note — two-tier rollout: MARKETPLACE label applies to ALL third-party items immediately (brand equity protection — non-negotiable, multiple complaints received). NEW and ONLINE ONLY labels apply to slow-moving own-brand items in a partial test first, then expand based on CTR and ATC data once GA4 events are live.
Marketplace Rollout
All marketplace items — full and immediate. Brand equity is at risk.
NEW / ONLINE ONLY Rollout
Partial — slow-moving own-brand SKUs first. Expand after CTR validation.
Benchmark
Home Centre KSA: identical label system. "MARKETPLACE", "NEW", "ONLINE ONLY", "BEST PRICE" all visible in live screenshots.
Pre-condition
GA4 CTR events live before NEW/ONLINE ONLY labels are tested. Marketplace label deploys independently — no measurement gate.
📧 15. Klaviyo Channel Analysis — Last 30 Days (Jun 2026)
Last refreshed: Jun 22, 2026
Klaviyo account health rated "Poor" across all 4 metrics. However this average is misleading — it is almost entirely dragged down by email underperformance. WhatsApp campaigns are performing at 3× industry benchmark. The two channels must be evaluated separately.

0.32%

0.01%
1 in 10,000 recipients places an order

▼ SAR 0.45 vs prior period · Rated Poor

WhatsApp vs Email — Revenue by Channel (Last 30 Days)

WhatsApp Revenue (3 sends)
SAR 215,824
Avg SAR 71,941 per campaign send
Email Revenue (4 sends)
SAR 34,417
Avg SAR 8,604 per campaign send
WhatsApp vs Email Ratio
6.3 : 1
WhatsApp outperforms email 6× per send
CampaignChannelDateRecipientsOpen RateClick RateRevenueGrade
vouchers campaignWhatsAppJun 1530,63447.74%2.35%SAR 103,721★★★★★
mega_sale_2026WhatsAppJun 1127,22549.03%6.63%SAR 93,955★★★★★
fathers dayWhatsAppJun 2124,53341.00%2.17%SAR 18,148★★★★
mega saleEmailJun 1436,3529.98%0.22%SAR 21,737★★★
Recliners 2EmailJun 2047,92523.62%0.40%SAR 12,680★★★
sofa setEmailJun 214,69238.06%0.65%SAR 0★★ ⚠️
Coffee table sets 2EmailJun 185,05541.03%0.67%SAR 0★★ ⚠️
RD4 (x2 drafts) — RiyadhEmailUNSENT———SAR 0 (sitting idle)⚠️ Send now
rd4 test / rd4 test_responseWhatsAppJun 222–366–75%0%SAR 0Internal test

5 Findings That Must Change Strategy Now

1.

WhatsApp is the business — email is a support channel

SAR 215,824 from 3 WhatsApp sends vs SAR 34,417 from 4 email sends. WhatsApp generates 6.3× more revenue per campaign. The Klaviyo "Poor" account rating is misleading — it averages email failure into WhatsApp excellence. These channels must be reported and optimized separately. WhatsApp send frequency should increase immediately.

2.

The email open-to-click gap is the core problem — not list quality

Sofa Set email: 38% open rate → SAR 0 revenue. Coffee Table Sets: 41% open rate → SAR 0 revenue. People are opening — subject lines work. Nothing inside the email makes them click. This is a content, CTA, and offer problem. Fix email body creative before sending more campaigns. Sending more broken emails burns subscriber goodwill.

3.

mega_sale_2026 (Jun 11 WhatsApp) is what caused the Klaviyo Shopify spike

The SAR 93,955 WhatsApp send on June 11 — the same day as the grid rollout — is what showed up as the "Klaviyo surge" in the Shopify channel data. The timeline is now fully clear. Grid rolled out Jun 11, mega_sale WhatsApp sent Jun 11, Klaviyo revenue spiked Jun 11. Two independent events, same day.

4.

Two Riyadh email drafts are sitting unsent while paid social is down

RD4 campaigns targeting Riyadh customers are drafted and ready as of Jun 22 but not sent. Riyadh is 24.5% of active users — the #2 city. While Meta and Google campaigns are underperforming, Klaviyo is the channel you control. Send the Riyadh drafts this week. Every day they sit idle is SAR 8,000–21,000 in estimated lost email revenue.

5.

mega_sale_2026 click rate of 6.63% is 3× WhatsApp industry benchmark

WhatsApp marketing industry benchmark is 1.5–2.5% click rate. At 6.63%, the mega_sale_2026 campaign performed nearly 3× above benchmark. Analyze exactly what made this campaign work — the offer structure, the message copy, the CTA, the send time — and replicate it as a template for all future WhatsApp sends.

Revised Channel Strategy — Based on Data

WhatsApp — Scale Immediately
Current sends/month~3
Recommended sends/month6–8
Revenue per send (avg)SAR 71,941
Projected monthly at 6 sendsSAR ~431,000
Template to replicatemega_sale_2026 (6.63% CTR)
List size~27–30K recipients
Email — Fix Before Sending More
Open rate problemmega sale: 9.98% (very low)
Click rate problemAll campaigns: <1%
Revenue problemSofa Set + Coffee Table: SAR 0
What to fix firstEmail body CTA and offer clarity
Priority unsentRD4 Riyadh drafts — send this week
Test 22 priorityEmail list growth = top priority
Test 22 (Klaviyo exit-intent capture) is now the #1 priority test in the entire roadmap. WhatsApp list of ~30K generates SAR 215K in 30 days = SAR 7.19/recipient/month. Every new subscriber captured via homepage exit-intent = SAR 7+ in recoverable monthly revenue. At 3% capture rate on 600K monthly sessions = 18,000 new subs/month = +SAR 129,000/month in additional WhatsApp revenue potential — without spending a single riyal on paid media.
🚀 16. 30-Day A/B Test Sprint Plan (Jul 4 – Aug 2, 2026)
Last refreshed: Jul 4, 2026
ℹ️ Assumptions used to build this sprint — verify on Day 1

This plan assumes only Test 09 (category grid) is confirmed live, and Tests 31–32 are gated on GA4 CTR events per the document as of Jun 22. Status of Tests 01–08 and 10–30 between Jun 22 and Jul 4 is not confirmed in available data. Day 1 includes a status audit to correct sequencing if any of these have already shipped.

Corrected Baseline & Benchmark-Referenced Targets (Jul 4, 2026)

This sprint's metrics are now tied to the corrected traffic baseline (~2.0–2.5M sessions/month, confirmed by GA4 + Shopify ShopifyQL cross-check — see History Timeline, Section 21) rather than the original document's ~600K/month assumption.

MetricCurrent (Jul 4, 2026)Reference benchmarkSource & confidence30-day target
Sessions/month~2.0–2.5M—Confirmed: GA4 + Shopify ShopifyQL, Jan 1–Jul 4, 2026 (within ~17% of each other)Maintain; not a test target
Conversion Rate (order-based)0.143% (Shopify orders/sessions)0.8%–1.6% (global furniture/home-decor e-commerce)Global, not KSA-specific. Sources: fyresite.com, vividworks.com, chatboq.com (Jan–Mar 2026). No verified KSA-specific furniture CVR benchmark found.Directional improvement toward the low end of the global range; treat as aspirational given the benchmark gap, not a committed number
Add-to-Cart Rate2.54% (GA4 events, Jan–Jul)9.0%–9.5% (Saudi e-commerce, all categories)KSA-specific but NOT furniture-specific. Source: ECDB.com, 2025 data.Meaningful directional increase; the all-category benchmark is likely optimistic for high-consideration furniture, so treat as a ceiling reference, not a like-for-like target
Cart Abandonment~76.5% (implied: 1 − 3.02% cart→purchase, approximate)76.0%–76.5% (Saudi e-commerce, all categories)KSA-specific but NOT furniture-specific. Source: ECDB.com, 2025 data.Currently roughly in line with the all-category KSA benchmark — this is the metric with the smallest confirmed gap to close
Bounce RateNot yet re-pulled from the new GA4 export — the 31.8% figure in Section 2 is from the original Jun 13 analysis and has not been refreshed against Jan–Jul data.No verified benchmark found for this metric specifically—Pull current bounce rate before setting a numeric target — flagged as an open item
📌 Honest gap: I found no verified, KSA-specific, furniture-industry benchmark for conversion rate, add-to-cart rate, or bounce rate. The targets above use the closest verifiable references (global furniture CVR benchmarks; Saudi all-category ATC/abandonment benchmarks) with the scope mismatch stated plainly rather than presented as an exact match.

Run in parallel — different page zones, no overlap. Read out via CTR/ATC/bounce proxy metrics; full purchase-CR confirmation follows in later weeks.

Days 22–28 (Jul 25–31) — Week 4: Read-Outs & Rollout

No new tests launched. Apply the Rollout Decision Matrix (Section 11) to every test that has reached its minimum signal threshold. This is also the earliest point most Week 1 tests (started Jul 5) reach the 14-day minimum duration for a valid read.

Ship
Winners
Roll out to 100% traffic per matrix thresholds
Extend
Promising, low sample
Keep running past Day 30 for purchase-CR confirmation
Kill
Negative signal
Revert per matrix's "if negative" column

Days 29–30 (Aug 1–2) — Reset

  • Update the Weekly Reporting scorecard (Section 12) with Day 30 actuals vs the Jul 4 baseline.
  • Queue the next cycle: Elevarre section (12), Qeen AI personalization (13), shoppable room scene (27), currency auto-detect (29) — the remaining tests not covered in this sprint.
  • Reassess the significance timeline: tests still short of the 1,000-conversion/variant threshold move into a second 30-day extension rather than being force-declared.
🛒 17. PLP Track — Product Card & PDP Testing (Added Jul 4, 2026)
Last refreshed: Jul 4, 2026
ℹ️ Why this is a separate track

This work is on collection (PLP) and product (PDP) pages, not the homepage. Running it in parallel with — but reported separately from — the homepage 30-Day Sprint keeps attribution clean: a metric move on the homepage sprint shouldn't get confused with a metric move here, and vice versa.

PLP-01 — PLP Refresh: Delivery-Text Fix + New Product Card Design

Variant A — Current design (attached now)

Package Deal badge + gift-promo overlay + uniform red delivery text — inconsistent badge system, no per-SKU Tabby monthly price shown

Control B — Shared previously (proposed new card design)

Online Exclusive badge + per-SKU Tabby monthly price + delivery text color-coded by speed (green = 7-14 days, red = 1-2 months)

📌 Labeling note: labeled exactly as requested (Variant A = image attached in this message, Control B = image shared earlier). This reverses the usual convention (Control = baseline/A, Variant = new/B) — flagging in case that was a slip rather than intentional, since it affects how results should be read later.
Scope
Bundled release: (1) fix for the delivery-message color bug so all scenarios correctly show green/red by delivery speed, shipped alongside (2) the new product card design (Online Exclusive badge, per-SKU Tabby monthly price, color-coded delivery message)
Reporting basis
Combined impact only — "PLP refresh" as one result, not attributed to either change individually (agreed Jul 4). A future isolated test would be needed to know if the card design alone is worth extending to other collections.
Primary metrics
PLP → PDP click-through rate, add-to-cart rate (fast proxy signals, readable within days)
Secondary / monitored, not primary
Tabby order growth % — tracked via existing backend order data, not treated as this test's primary success metric
📌 Not yet confirmed: PLP session volume, current Tabby-at-checkout rate, and whether this card redesign covers the full PLP template or only the Confa/Roots product line. Needed to size how fast a reliable read is achievable.

Upcoming — PDP A/B Tests (dates not yet set)

Placeholder for the PDP testing track referenced Jul 4. To be scheduled once the design system and PLP-01 have firmer timelines. Any PDP image or data changes must follow the Merchandising Listing Guidelines — see the open item below before these tests are scoped.

  • PDP gallery structure and ordering
  • Product data presentation (dimensions, material, title format)
  • Any PDP-level Tabby/BNPL treatment, if warranted after PLP-01's read

Design Governance Inputs (from uploaded guideline decks)

GuidelineApplies toKey rule
Marketing Banner GuidelinesHomepage banner/promo blocks (1, 2, 5, 6, 8, 9, 13)Design 70% / text ≤30%; product sharp, no crop/blur/filter; offer stated plainly; extendable campaigns omit end date, fixed campaigns always show it; every banner must match a defined theme
Merchandising Listing GuidelinesPDP product images & dataProduct gallery in a fixed image set, 1:1 square aspect ratio; dimensions always as D × W × H in cm; titles clear and unique
📌 Internal inconsistency in the Merchandising Listing Guidelines deck: Slide 3 specifies a 6-image gallery set, while Slide 7 and Slide 8 both say 7-image set. This needs to be resolved with whoever owns that deck before it's used as the sourcing spec for PDP tests — sourcing against the wrong count wastes a merchandising cycle.
📌 Calendar cross-check: the uploaded 2026 marketing calendar could not be reliably mapped to specific July/August campaign dates from its extracted text (the OCR output scrambled month groupings). Given how the Jul 24 Tabby campaign already factors into PLP-01's timing, confirm directly whether any other calendar campaign lands inside the Jul 4 – Aug 2 window before finalizing the design-system and PDP schedule.
🧮 18. Data & Methodology Notes (Added Jul 4, 2026)
Last refreshed: Jul 4, 2026

Corrections applied in this update

✏️

Test 30 revenue estimate

Original: "+1,500 cart adds → +600 orders → +SAR 1.37M/period." The document's own funnel conversion (Cart→Checkout 48.5% × Checkout→Purchase 13.7% = 6.64% cart-to-purchase) implies ~100 orders (~SAR 228K), not 600 orders. The 600-order figure implies a ~40% cart-to-purchase rate not supported elsewhere in the document. Both figures are now shown in Test 30's detail card, with the funnel-consistent estimate labeled as the conservative base case and the original figure labeled as an optimistic upper bound.

✏️

Test 22 / Success Criteria "per year" label

18,000 subs × 3% flow CR × SAR 2,282 AOV = SAR 1,232,280 is the revenue from one month's subscriber cohort, not an annual figure as originally labeled. Relabeled in both locations. This also sits alongside a separate WhatsApp-based estimate (SAR 129,000/month from the same 18K subs, calculated on a different basis in Section 15) — the two are not yet reconciled and should be treated as two different lenses on the same subscriber growth, not additive.

Open items not resolved by this update

  • Exact ship status of Tests 01–08 and 10–30 as of Jul 4 — resolved by the Day 1 status audit in Section 16, not by this document alone.
  • The "Weekly Revenue YTD: SAR 35.2M" label in the header is ambiguous — unclear whether this is a weekly figure or a YTD total labeled inconsistently. Recommend clarifying before this figure is quoted externally.
  • Full purchase-conversion statistical significance (1,000 conversions/variant) is not achievable within 30 days at current traffic and CR for any single sequential test — see the note added to Section 10. Treat Day 30 rollout decisions on proxy metrics as provisional pending longer-run purchase-CR confirmation.

22,584 of 32,409 active products are missing critical metadata or have structural problems that suppress organic ranking and click-through. Full per-SKU tool with team check-ins and CSV export is a separate file — link at the bottom of this tab.

📌 Duplicate titles (6,517) are the most consequential single finding here — Google picks one instance and effectively ignores the rest, which directly suppresses organic visibility across a large share of the catalog.

By Brand Group

GroupActive ProductsIssuesIssue Rate
Ashley Homestore4,9234,21386%

Work Priority

🔴 Tier 1 — Critical

No SEO Title, No SEO Description, No Image, Corrupt HTML. Google cannot rank or display these properly — zero organic benefit until fixed.

🟠 Tier 2 — High

No Body Content, Title >60 chars, Duplicate Title. Weakens relevance signals and causes cannibalization in search results.

🟡 Tier 3 — Medium

Title <30 chars. Missed keyword opportunity — fix after Tiers 1 and 2 are cleared.

Full SEO Tool

Per-SKU priority lists, search/filter, CSV export, and the team check-in tracker live in a separate hosted tool (kept separate because it's a React app, not vanilla JS/HTML like this roadmap — see the earlier discussion on why these don't share one file). Hosted at: haithamhassan-alrugaib.github.io/CRO_SEO/alrugaib_seo_tracker.html

Open Full SEO Dashboard →
